question: 1. Fetch all employees using OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE cycle.
2. Write a PL/SQL block to fetch employee name and salary and display them.
3. Display employees whose salary is greater than 3000 using a cursor.
4. Calculate the total salary of all employees using a cursor and a variable.
5. Count the total number of records processed by the cursor using %ROWCOUNT.
6. Display all employee records using a Cursor FOR LOOP.
7. Write a PL/SQL block to display employee names and jobs using a Cursor FOR LOOP.
8. Write a PL/SQL block to display only employees from department 10 using a Cursor FOR LOOP.
9. Calculate the total salary of the company using Cursor FOR LOOP logic.
10. Display employees whose salary is greater than the company average using Cursor FOR LOOP.
11. Declare a parameterized cursor that takes a deptno and displays its employees.
12. Fetch employees whose salary is greater than a value passed as a cursor parameter.
13. Display employees based on a specific Job Role passed to a parameterized cursor.
14. Pass multiple parameters (deptno and job) to a cursor and display matching records.
15. Calculate the total salary of a specific department passed as a parameter.
16. Update the salary of employees using a cursor with the FOR UPDATE clause.
17. Increase salary by 10% for specific employees using the WHERE CURRENT OF clause.
18. Delete employees with a salary less than 2000 using an updatable cursor.
19. Update the Job title of employees row-by-row using an updatable cursor.
20. Fetch employee data, modify a value, and then display updated result within same loop.
21. Display the top 3 highest-paid employees using a cursor.
22. Simulate pagination by fetching only rows 5 to 7.
23. Salary increments based on roles (CLERK 5%, MANAGER 10%).
24. Copy data from EMP to backup table using cursor-based row processing.
